Engineering proposals · ExtendSim 2026 Pro

Driving block compilation & scripting from COM automation

To
ExtendSim block engineering — ANDRITZ / Imagine That
From
Duke Systems AB — COM automation layer for ExtendSim 2026 Pro
Date
2026-07-18
Re
Two related proposals — the Equation and Python Bridge blocks

We drive ExtendSim 2026 Pro over COM/OLE (ExtendSim.Application) to build and validate models programmatically. Two blocks currently have no automation path for a core operation — compiling an equation, and setting a Python script. Both gaps share one root cause, and one fix.

The common mechanism

app.Execute() runs in system context, which cannot call a block's compiler or reach its private STAT arrays. But a dialog-item handler runs in block context — and setting a dialog variable from COM fires that item's on <var> handler. That single bridge underlies both proposals below; we verified it independently with a throwaway block (a handler fired from COM wrote a sentinel that COM read back).

PART A

Equation block — compile & validate

An automation client cannot compile or validate an Equation block's equation. The block compiles at on OK, on CheckData, on Simulate, and via the compile-button message — each reached only through the GUI or a full run, reporting syntax errors through a modal UserError dialog that deadlocks a headless host.

A·01The proposed handler

A checkbox CompileAndReport whose handler translates the text, compiles with the silent primitive, and writes the verdict to COM-readable globals:

on CompileAndReport
{
    integer compileError;
    EQ_TranslateEquation();                    // fill EQ_TranslatedEquationText
    compileError = EquationCompileDynamicVariablesSilent("iVars_Values", "oVars_Values",
        EQ_TranslatedEquationText, EQ_CompiledEquation, EQUATION_TAB_ORDER);
    if (compileError == 0) { global0 = 1; globalStr0 = ""; }
    else                    { global0 = 0; globalStr0 = "Equation compile error code " + compileError; }
}
SetDialogVariable(blockNum, "EQ_EquationText",  "outCon0 = 123 + 456;", 0, 0)
SetDialogVariable(blockNum, "CompileAndReport", 1,                      0, 0)  # fires handler
ok = Request("global0")   # "1" = OK, "0" = error   err = Request("globalStr0")

A·02Result

✓ Works — valid equation

For outCon0 = 123 + 456; the client reads global0 = "1", globalStr0 = "". End-to-end — no dialog, no run, no wedge. We built this by copying Value.lbr, adding the checkbox, appending the handler, and recompiling your real Equation block — all 13 includes resolved and it compiled cleanly.

⚠ Hangs — invalid equation, out-of-run

EquationCompileDynamicVariablesSilent(…) hangs when invoked outside a running simulation — no dialog appears (confirmed with a UI-Automation watcher running throughout), so nothing can be dismissed and ExtendSim must be force-restarted. The loud EQ_Compile(FALSE) path wedged the same way. Reproduced with a syntax error (outCon0 = 1 +;) and an undefined variable (outCon0 = zzz;).

Hypothesis: the compile primitives assume a run/init context (populated iVars_Values/oVars_Values, completed CheckData/InitSim); on a freshly placed block the error path enters a bad state and hangs. During a real run the same compiler reports errors cleanly, as a dismissable dialog.

A·03Requests

  1. Make EquationCompileDynamicVariablesSilent(…) robust out-of-run — return a nonzero error code on invalid input regardless of run/init state, never blocking. This alone makes the handler above a complete compile-and-validate API.
  2. Consider a first-class OLE entry point, e.g. CompileEquationBlock(blockNum) → {ok, errorText}, with the human-readable error text the loud path already produces.
PART B

Python Bridge block — set the script

An automation client cannot set a Python Bridge block's script. The code lives in dsPythonCode[], a block-private STAT array the dialog API cannot address (SetDialogVariable(id,"dsPythonCode",row,0) → “Row or column index out of range for static variable”), and the only entry path is the interactive editor.

B·01The proposed handlers

The same bridge applies: a handler running in block context can index dsPythonCode[]. One line per element sidesteps the fact that a ModL string literal cannot carry newlines — the client iterates lines and sets a one-line buffer per row.

on PY_SizeTrig   { MakeArray(dsPythonCode, PY_SizeTrig); global0 = GetDimension(dsPythonCode); }
on PY_InjectTrig { dsPythonCode[PY_LineIndex] = PY_InjectBuf; global0 = GetDimension(dsPythonCode); }
on PY_ReadTrig   { globalStr0 = dsPythonCode[PY_LineIndex]; }

B·02Result

✓ Works — multi-line injection

On a purpose-built block declaring String dsPythonCode[]; and these handlers, a COM client sized the array and injected three lines one-per-index:

read back → ["import numpy as np", "x = np.arange(10)", "result = x.sum()"]
match = TRUE

Arbitrary multi-line Python injected from COM into dsPythonCode[] and read back verbatim — no dialog, no wedge.

B·03Scope of the proof

  • Proven: COM → in-block handler → populate dsPythonCode[] (arbitrary multi-line) → read back exact, on a block using the same String-array pattern the Python Bridge uses.
  • Not yet on the stock block: we did not hand-patch the real Python Bridge (its large blob contains non-ASCII bytes that make in-place source surgery risky) — but the same modify-and-recompile technique succeeded on your real Equation block (Part A).
  • Execution is separate: injection is not execution. To run, the block must register the code (it already uses PythonEditorAddRelatedCode(scriptID, dsPythonCode, append)), with Python initialized and a trigger configured.

B·04Requests

  1. A COM/OLE way to set the script — make dsPythonCode[] settable through a supported entry point, or add inject handlers like the above to the stock Python Bridge.
  2. Register + optionally execute in one call — e.g. SetPythonScript(blockNum, lines[]) → {ok, errorText} that stores the lines and calls PythonEditorAddRelatedCode(…), reporting any Python syntax error back to the client.
Duke Systems AB · prepared 2026-07-18 · Handler sources and the working blocks are available on request. Happy to demo both proposals over a call.
